perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will take commercially reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include at least the same degree of care that the receiving party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'commercially reasonable' significantly lowers the standard of care. Also the removal of 'which measures shall include at least the same degree of care that the receiving party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature' lowers the bar for protecting information to some undefined 'measures'. This creates a legal contradiction because the 'commercially reasonable standard' is the standard required in Trade Secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'commercially reasonable' clause, the agreement no longer aligns with the standard of care required to protect trade secrets under the DTSA and state trade secret laws. The act states that a party should make reasonable efforts to protect information, and the original contract reflected this idea. Now it no longer does.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The removal of "commercially reasonable measures" and the specific degree of care significantly lowers the standard required to protect confidential information. The original contract reflected the idea of 'reasonable efforts,' aligning with trade secret law's expectation of reasonable measures to safeguard trade secrets, as stated in the Law Explanation. By removing this standard, the changed text contradicts the commonly held standard of Trade Secret law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not specifically discuss 'commercially reasonable measures', the Law explanation states the original contract aligned with the idea, and the modified version no longer does, which is a serious issue.

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will take all reasonable precautions to prevent any trading in securities of the other party, including advising its Associates who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this letter agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information of the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information, and directing such Associates to comply with those restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware, and will take precautions to prevent any trading in securities of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This omission weakens the safeguards against insider trading. By removing the requirement to advise associates about securities law restrictions, the agreement no longer adequately addresses the legal obligations to prevent insider trading, potentially creating legal risk. The precautions must be 'reasonable' and the contract removed the requirement to advise associates about the nature of insider trading.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. By removing the specific requirements to educate associates about insider trading restrictions, the agreement provides less assurance that the parties will comply with these laws. The precautions must be 'reasonable' and the contract removed the requirement to advise associates about the nature of insider trading.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires each party to advise its associates about restrictions imposed by United States securities laws. The changed text removes this explicit requirement, only stating that each party will 'take precautions to prevent any trading in securities'. This omission weakens the safeguards against insider trading, as the specific duty to educate associates is removed. According to the law explanation, section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, and the removed requirement assures compliance with this law. The edited version provides less assurance that the parties will comply with these laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, confirms that manipulative and deceptive devices are unlawful. By removing the explicit measure of advising associates, the modified contract increases the risk of non-compliance, creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party, upon the disclosing party’s request, will promptly return all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party, together with all copies, or at the option of the receiving party, certify in writing that all such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party, upon the disclosing party’s request, will return all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the option for the receiving party to certify in writing that the confidential information has been destroyed. In legal terms, a certificate of destruction is evidence that steps were taken to maintain confidentiality. Deleting this option reduces the protection of the disclosing party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 37(e)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 37(e) addresses the failure to preserve electronically stored information in litigation. While this agreement concerns all Confidential Information and not just ESI, the principle remains that documentation of destruction is legally relevant. Removing the certification option increases the risk of spoliation claims should the Confidential Information be relevant in future litigation. The certification of destruction is a step taken to avoid the implication that data was destroyed to avoid future litigation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the option for the receiving party to provide written certification that confidential information has been destroyed, upon request. The LLM explanation argues that this increases the risk of spoliation claims, should confidential information be relevant in future litigation, because documentation of destruction is legally relevant. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on compelling disclosure or discovery, it still implies the need for documentation. The original text contains a protection for the disclosing party that the modified text removes. This constitutes a legal contradiction, since the modified text removes steps to maintain confidentiality for the disclosing party.
